petitioner_background: The petitioner is a self-represented individual from China seeking classification as an alien of extraordinary ability under section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Act as a painter, teacher, and photographer. He has been residing in the United States since November 14, 1996, and filed the petition on September 30, 2005 (more than eight years after his arrival). His claimed accomplishments include various art awards and recognitions received primarily in China during the early-to-mid 1990s.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted 13 items: (1) Certificate for first prize at the 'Kiafen Excellent Folk Fine Art Exhibition' for 'Scenery Traditional Chinese Painting' (November 1990); (2) Certificate acknowledging contribution to the second session of Henan Art Festival 'Henan Folk Art Exhibition' (October 1990); (3) Fill-in-the-blank 'Certificate of Award' for 1st prize at the 1st International Popular Science Fine Arts, Calligraphy and Photograph Contest (undated); (4) Certificate from the Chinese Portrait Photography Association awarding title of 'Master of Chinese Portrait Photography' (1992); (5) Certificate from the Chinese Photographers' Works Collection Committee awarding title of 'Chinese Outstanding Photographer' (1991); (6) Fill-in-the-blank certificate from the 'Bureau of Chinese Culture Department' stating 'Flower and Bird' was selected for the 'Eighth National Fine Art Exhibit' (1994); (7) Certificate from the 'Culture Bureau of Anhui Province' for first place for 'Sense of Sea' at the Fine Arts, Calligraphic Works and Photography of Social Culture System Exhibit of Anhui Province (December 1995); (8) Certificate for first place for 'The Spring of the Peony' at the 'Second Session of Industrial Arts Exhibition' (September 1993); (9) Honor Certificate for Golden Award at the 'Ninth Nation Painting Exhibition' (1992); (10) Honor Certificate from the Cultural Bureau of Anhui Province for third place for 'The Taste' at the ''New Century, New Work' Fine Art and Photographic Work Competition of Anhui' (1991); (11) Certificate for second prize in the 'Fine Arts, Calligraphy and Photography Contest of Anhui Province' (August 1994); (12) Certificate from the 'State Department of China' stating the petitioner received a special government allowance (1992); (13) Photograph showing various trophies and award certificates (text and inscriptions illegible).
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found multiple deficiencies. First, the translations accompanying the award certificates were not certified as required by 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(3). Second, items 1, 2, 7, 10, and 11 reflected local or provincial recognition rather than national or international recognition. Third, for all 13 items, there was no evidence of contemporaneous publicity surrounding the awards or evidence showing they command a substantial level of recognition; the record lacked evidence of the number of recipients, the criteria for granting the awards, the level of expertise of those considered, and the number of individuals eligible to compete. There was no supporting documentation from awarding entities or print media establishing that the awards are nationally or internationally recognized. Additionally, the petitioner submitted no evidence of significant awards in China or the United States after 1995, indicating that any acclaim earned in China during the 1990s had not been sustained.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: On appeal, the petitioner stated that he is a member of the 'Fine Artists' Association of China' and the 'Jilin Fine Artists' Association.' In response to the director's notice of intent to deny, the petitioner submitted documents entitled 'The Chinese Artist Association Introduction' and 'Chinese Artist Association Regulation.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO noted that to meet this criterion, the petitioner must show that the association requires outstanding achievement as an essential condition for admission, and that members must be selected at the national or international level rather than the local/regional level. The record included no evidence of the petitioner's membership credentials for either association. Going on record without supporting documentary evidence is insufficient to meet the burden of proof, citing Matter of Soffici. The AAO could not accept 'The Chinese Artist Association Introduction' and 'Chinese Artist Association Regulation' because the English translations were not certified per 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(3), the source of the documents was not properly identified, and there was no evidence demonstrating that the Fine Artists' Association of China and the Chinese Artist Association are the same entity. The petitioner offered no evidence showing membership in an association requiring outstanding achievement of its members as judged by recognized experts at the national or international level.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted several images of what were alleged to be his artistic creations. In response to the director's notice of intent to deny, the petitioner submitted four images of works entitled 'Welcome Spring,' 'Spring Flavor,' 'Garden,' and 'Huge Mythical Bird' which he claimed were published in The People's Daily Newspaper, and an image of his painting 'Spring of Peace' which he claimed was published in Chinese Folk Art Magazine.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found that without further evidence, it was not established that the petitioner's works were among those shown in the images. The images were not accompanied by contemporaneous evidence (such as an event program or art brochure) indicating the specific exhibition or showcase in which they appeared. The record contained no evidence showing that the claimed works were actually published in the People's Daily Newspaper or Chinese Folk Art Magazine, and no evidence identifying the date, month, and year of the specific issues in which they allegedly appeared. Going on record without supporting documentary evidence is insufficient, citing Matter of Soffici. There was no evidence demonstrating that the petitioner's works had been displayed at significant national or international venues, no indication the works had been featured alongside those of artists with national or international reputations, and no demonstration of regular participation in shows or exhibitions at exclusive venues devoted largely to the display of his artwork alone.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted an 'ISC 1997 Certificate' issued by the 'Department of Art Products Cultural Market Development Center' of the Chinese Cultural Ministry authorizing the petitioner 'to promote, introduce, sell, and export through domestic and international cultural markets.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the record included no official financial documentation (such as payroll records or income tax forms) showing the petitioner's actual earnings for any given period of time. The plain language of the criterion requires evidence of high remuneration 'in relation to others in the field,' and there was no evidence showing that the petitioner had earned a level of compensation significantly higher than others in his field. Nor was there any indication that the petitioner earned a level of compensation placing him among the highest paid artists in the United States or China.

outcome_reasoning: The AAO dismissed the appeal, finding that the petitioner failed to demonstrate receipt of a major internationally recognized award or satisfaction of at least three of the regulatory criteria necessary to establish sustained acclaim. The record did not establish that whatever acclaim the petitioner had in China had been sustained since his entry into the United States in 1996 — more than eight years before filing — despite having ample time to establish a reputation. Review of the record did not establish that the petitioner had distinguished himself to such an extent that he had achieved sustained national or international acclaim or placed him within the small percentage at the very top of his field. Additionally, beyond the director's decision, the petitioner failed to comply with 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(5) because the record included no clear evidence (letters from prospective employers, contracts, or a statement of plans) that the petitioner was coming to the U.S. to continue work in the area of expertise. Though the petitioner indicated on Form I-290B that a brief and/or evidence would be submitted within 30 days, more than seven months later the AAO had received nothing further.
legal_citations: Section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act, 8 U.S.C. § 1153(b)(1)(A)
Matter of Treasure Craft of California, 14 I&N Dec. 190 (Reg. Comm. 1972)
Spencer Enterprises, Inc. v. United States, 229 F. Supp. 2d 1025 (E.D. Cal. 2001), aff'd, 345 F.3d 683 (9th Cir. 2003)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: Multiple procedural issues: (1) English language translations of foreign documents (award certificates and association documents) were not certified as complete and accurate by a competent translator as required by 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(3); (2) the petitioner indicated on Form I-290B that a brief and/or evidence would be submitted to the AAO within 30 days, but more than seven months later nothing further had been received; (3) the source of the Chinese Artist Association documents was not properly identified; (4) the petitioner failed to submit evidence required under 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(5) that he was coming to the U.S. to continue work in the area of expertise. No fraud or misrepresentation findings.
